nodes.label: C1.2 Cell Respiration
nodes.description: Cell respiration is the controlled release of energy from organic compounds to produce ATP. This overarching topic is divided into several major interconnected concepts that occur across different cellular compartments.

nodes.label: Glycolysis
nodes.description: Glycolysis is an anaerobic process occurring in the cytoplasm. It breaks down a 6-carbon glucose molecule into two 3-carbon pyruvate molecules.

nodes.label: Phosphorylation (Glycolysis)
nodes.description: First, in phosphorylation, hexokinase uses two ATP molecules to add phosphate groups to glucose, making it unstable.

nodes.label: Lysis (Glycolysis)
nodes.description: During lysis, this hexose bisphosphate splits into two triose phosphate molecules.

nodes.label: Oxidation (Glycolysis)
nodes.description: In the oxidation phase, triose phosphate loses hydrogen, reducing NAD+ to NADH.

nodes.label: ATP Formation (Glycolysis)
nodes.description: Finally, enzymes transfer phosphate groups directly to ADP, generating a net yield of two ATP via substrate-level phosphorylation.

nodes.label: The Link Reaction
nodes.description: If oxygen is present, pyruvate enters the mitochondrial matrix via transport proteins. Here, it undergoes oxidative decarboxylation.

nodes.label: Pyruvate Transport
nodes.description: Pyruvate enters the mitochondrial matrix via transport proteins.

nodes.label: Oxidative Decarboxylation (Link Reaction)
nodes.description: Pyruvate loses a carbon atom as carbon dioxide and is simultaneously oxidized, reducing NAD+ to NADH.

nodes.label: Acetyl CoA Formation
nodes.description: The remaining 2-carbon acetyl group binds to Coenzyme A, forming Acetyl CoA.

nodes.label: The Krebs Cycle
nodes.description: Located in the mitochondrial matrix, the Krebs Cycle is a cyclical mechanism of oxidation.

nodes.label: Acetyl CoA Entry (Krebs Cycle)
nodes.description: Acetyl CoA delivers its 2-carbon acetyl group to the cycle.

nodes.label: Citrate Formation
nodes.description: The acetyl group from Acetyl CoA binds with 4C Oxaloacetate, forming 6C Citrate.

nodes.label: 1st Oxidative Decarboxylation (Krebs)
nodes.description: Citrate undergoes oxidative decarboxylation, releasing carbon dioxide and leading to a 5C molecule.

nodes.label: 2nd Oxidative Decarboxylation (Krebs)
nodes.description: Further oxidative decarboxylation occurs, releasing carbon dioxide and resulting in a 4C molecule.

nodes.label: Regeneration of Oxaloacetate
nodes.description: The 4C molecule is regenerated back into Oxaloacetate, completing the cycle.

nodes.label: Decarboxylase Enzymes (Krebs)
nodes.description: Throughout the Krebs Cycle, decarboxylase enzymes remove carbon dioxide.

nodes.label: Dehydrogenase Enzymes (Krebs)
nodes.description: Dehydrogenase enzymes reduce both NAD+ and FAD into NADH and FADH2 during the Krebs Cycle.

nodes.label: ATP Production (Krebs Cycle)
nodes.description: A small amount of ATP is also produced via substrate-level phosphorylation within the Krebs Cycle.

nodes.label: The Electron Transport Chain
nodes.description: The ETC is a series of trans-membrane protein complexes embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane (cristae).

nodes.label: Electron Delivery (ETC)
nodes.description: NADH and FADH2 deliver high-energy electrons to the protein complexes of the ETC.

nodes.label: Proton Pumping (ETC)
nodes.description: As electrons are passed down the chain, they lose energy. This energy drives proton pumping, moving hydrogen ions from the matrix into the narrow intermembrane space, building a massive concentration gradient.

nodes.label: Oxygen as Final Electron Acceptor
nodes.description: At the end of the chain, molecular oxygen acts as the final electron acceptor, binding with electrons and free protons to form water, preventing the chain from backing up.

nodes.label: Chemiosmosis
nodes.description: Chemiosmosis is the mechanism that couples the proton gradient to ATP synthesis.

nodes.label: Proton Motive Force
nodes.description: The concentration gradient of hydrogen ions (protons) across the inner mitochondrial membrane builds, creating a proton motive force.

nodes.label: ATP Synthase
nodes.description: Because the inner membrane is impermeable to protons, they must diffuse back into the matrix through a specialized enzyme called ATP Synthase.

nodes.label: ATP Synthesis (Chemiosmosis)
nodes.description: The flow of protons through ATP Synthase causes its rotor subunit to turn, leading to a conformational change in the catalytic knob, where ADP binds with inorganic phosphate, resulting in massive ATP production.
